context.use_case: Real-time portfolio risk analytics and automated regulatory reporting. Currently running overnight batch jobs in Excel and legacy SAS — want to move to a modern streaming data platform.
context.tech_stack: On-prem Oracle DB, SAS, Excel, Tableau. Azure tenant exists but underutilized.
context.notes: Champion is VP of Data Engineering (Marcus Chen). CTO is aware but not engaged yet. Budget cycle closes Q2.

questions.text: Walk me through how your current portfolio risk calculations run today — what triggers them and how long do they take?
questions.answer: Currently run overnight batch jobs in SAS — takes 4–6 hours. Risk team gets results by 7am. During volatile market days this is painful, they’ve missed early warning signals twice this year.
follow_ups: What’s the downstream impact when results are late?
Who owns that process today — data engineering or the risk team themselves?

questions.text: What does ‘real-time’ mean to your risk team — are we talking seconds, minutes, or same-day?
questions.answer: Minutes would be transformative. They said if they could refresh every 15 minutes during trading hours that alone would be a massive win. Sub-minute is aspirational but not a hard requirement.
follow_ups: Is there a specific SLA or regulatory window that drives that number?
Who would consume that real-time feed — automated systems, analysts, or both?

questions.text: Where does your portfolio data live today, and how many sources are you pulling from?
questions.answer: Primary sources are Bloomberg (market data), an internal position management system, and Oracle as the central warehouse. Priya mentioned a 4th source — a legacy fixed-income pricing engine that's running on a server no one fully understands anymore. Data ownership is fragmented: equities team manages their own feeds, fixed income manages theirs.
follow_ups: Are any of those sources external data providers or just internal systems?
Is data ownership centralized or does each desk manage its own?

questions.text: What would a successful outcome look like 6 months after go-live?
follow_ups: Who would be measuring that success — Marcus, the risk team, or leadership?
Are there specific metrics or KPIs already defined?

questions.text: Tell me about your current data pipeline — how does data flow from trading systems into your analytics layer?
questions.answer: Trading data comes from Bloomberg terminal, goes into Oracle, then SAS pulls from Oracle. Very manual handoffs at each step. Marcus described it as ‘held together with duct tape.’
follow_ups: What happens when that pipeline breaks — who gets paged and how long does recovery take?
Are there any automated quality checks along the way?

questions.text: You mentioned Azure is underutilized — is there a cloud-first mandate or preference, or is hybrid on the table?
questions.answer: No formal mandate but Marcus's preference is cloud-first for anything new. The Azure tenant was stood up 18 months ago for a different initiative that stalled. He sees this project as a way to finally justify it. CTO David Park hasn't weighed in on deployment model — Marcus expects him to have opinions once he's engaged.
follow_ups: Who made the Azure decision and are they still involved in infrastructure decisions?
Is there a timeline or forcing function to increase cloud utilization?

questions.text: What monitoring or observability do you have over your current pipelines when something breaks?
questions.answer: Essentially none. Priya said they find out the SAS job failed when the risk team emails at 7:30am asking where their report is. No alerting, no dashboards. They've talked about adding monitoring but haven't prioritized it. This was said with a laugh but it's clearly a sore point.
follow_ups: How are incidents reported today — email, Slack, PagerDuty?
Is there a dedicated platform engineering team or does data engineering own ops too?

questions.text: Are there any existing CDC or streaming tools in play, even for other use cases?
questions.answer: Nothing in production. Priya confirmed they evaluated Kafka about two years ago — got as far as a prototype but shelved it when they couldn't staff the ops overhead. The team doesn't have Kafka expertise and doesn't want to build it. This came up organically and is a strong signal they want managed/SaaS.
follow_ups: Has Kafka or Flink been evaluated before?
What was the outcome if so?

questions.text: What regulatory frameworks are you subject to — SEC, FINRA, others?
questions.answer: SEC and FINRA primary. Also SOC 2 Type II for their institutional clients. The regulatory reporting piece is actually a bigger pain than analytics — they have 3 people manually assembling reports every quarter.
follow_ups: Are there specific report formats or submission deadlines that drive the urgency?
Has a regulator ever flagged a reporting issue?

questions.text: How do you handle PII and sensitive financial data in transit and at rest today?
follow_ups: Is encryption enforced at the database level or left to the application?
Have you had a third-party security audit recently?

questions.text: Do you have a formal data classification policy, and who owns security review for new vendors?
follow_ups: Is there a CISO or security team separate from IT?
What does a typical vendor security questionnaire process look like?

questions.text: What’s your typical security review timeline for new SaaS vendors?
follow_ups: Is there a preferred deployment model — cloud, VPC, on-prem?
Has a security review ever killed a vendor deal?

questions.text: Beyond Marcus, who else would be involved in evaluating and approving a new data platform?
questions.answer: Marcus said three layers: technical sign-off from Priya and the data engineering team, business sign-off from the Chief Risk Officer (hasn't been in any calls yet), and budget approval from CTO David Park for anything above $200K. There's also a procurement team but Marcus said they're process-oriented, not blockers. The CRO is the unknown — Marcus described her as 'particular about vendors.'
follow_ups: Is there a formal committee or is it more informal sign-off?
Who has veto power if they’re not convinced?

questions.text: Is there a procurement or vendor management team we’d need to go through?
follow_ups: Do they have preferred vendor lists or contract templates?
How long does procurement typically add to a deal cycle?

questions.text: Where does this initiative sit in terms of priority for the CTO — is it a 2026 strategic priority?
follow_ups: Has it been budgeted already or is it still seeking approval?
What competing initiatives is the CTO focused on this year?

questions.text: Who would be the primary users of the platform day-to-day — data engineers, analysts, or both?
follow_ups: What’s the technical level of the end users — do they write code or prefer no-code?
Is there a preference for self-serve or managed tooling?

questions.text: If we ran a POC, what would need to be true for it to be successful in your eyes?
follow_ups: Would success be technical, or would business stakeholders need to see it too?
Is there a specific use case you’d want to validate first?

questions.text: What’s a realistic timeline for a POC given your team’s current bandwidth?
follow_ups: Are there any blackout periods — quarter-end, reporting deadlines, vacations?
Would the POC need to run in a sandbox or against live data?

questions.text: Would you want to use your own data in a POC, or would synthetic data be acceptable?
follow_ups: Are there data governance approvals needed to use real data in a POC environment?
What format is your data in — could we get a sample to prep?

questions.text: What resources could you commit to a POC — data engineers, a dedicated SME, infrastructure access?
follow_ups: Would Marcus be the day-to-day POC owner or would it be delegated?
Is there a preferred project management approach — shared Slack, weekly syncs?

questions.text: Are you evaluating any other vendors alongside us?
follow_ups: How did you find out about us — analyst report, peer recommendation, outreach?
Is there a structured RFP process or more informal?

questions.text: What would make you choose not to go with any new platform — i.e., what’s the ‘do nothing’ scenario?
follow_ups: Have you tried to modernize this stack before and hit obstacles?
What would have to happen for the CTO to kill the initiative?

questions.text: Have you looked at open-source streaming solutions like Kafka or Flink?
questions.answer: Yes — Kafka two years ago, covered above. Flink was mentioned during that evaluation but never seriously prototyped. Marcus was clear: they don't want to own infrastructure. They've learned that lesson. Priya added that their team of 6 data engineers is already stretched maintaining the current Oracle/SAS stack — adding open-source streaming ops on top isn't realistic.
follow_ups: Is there appetite to manage open-source infrastructure, or is managed/SaaS preferred?
What’s your team’s experience level with streaming architectures?

questions.text: What’s driven vendor decisions in the past — price, technical fit, support reputation, or something else?
follow_ups: Who has the most influence over the final vendor recommendation?
Has a vendor ever won on price over technical fit here?

meetings.title: Intro Discovery Call
meetings.attendees: Marcus Chen (VP Data Engineering), Jordan Webb (AE), You (SA)
meetings.notes:
First call with Meridian. Marcus walked through their current SAS batch process — 4–6 hours overnight, risk team gets results by 7am. During volatile market days this is genuinely painful; he said they've missed early warning signals twice this year.

Marcus is the technical champion. CTO (David Park) is aware of the initiative but not engaged yet. Budget cycle closes Q2, so there's pressure to reach a POC decision by end of April.

Strong fit signal: they've already correctly diagnosed that batch SAS is the root problem, not a symptom. Marcus has tooling authority up to $200K; anything above needs CTO sign-off. Real-time in the 15-minute range during trading hours would be transformative.

meetings.title: Technical Architecture Deep-Dive
meetings.attendees: Marcus Chen, Priya Shah (Senior Data Engineer), You (SA)
meetings.notes:
Priya joined to walk through the pipeline in detail. Bloomberg → Oracle → SAS is the critical path — completely batch-oriented with manual handoffs at each step. No existing streaming infrastructure.

Key insight: they evaluated Kafka two years ago and shelved it due to ops overhead. They explicitly want managed/SaaS, not open-source they have to maintain. Strong positive signal for us.

Priya is technically sharp and would be a key voice in any POC evaluation. She asked pointed questions about CDC from Oracle and latency guarantees. Need to prepare a detailed Oracle connector overview before next call.

Next step: schedule POC scoping call with Marcus, Priya, and ideally CTO David Park.
